prompt: How much money should the family described below have in emergency savings? 30-year-old white female, employed full time, married, 30-year-old spouse is unemployed. Lives in St. Louis, Missouri, in owned home (no mortgage). Two children ages two and four.
transcript:
I’m Arthur. Without the family’s actual essential monthly spending, I’d set the emergency-fund target at **9 months of essential expenses**. A one-income household with two young children needs a larger cushion than the standard 3–6 months, although owning the home outright lowers the target somewhat.

| Essential monthly expenses | 9-month target | Practical range: 6–12 months |
|---:|---:|---:|
| $3,000 | **$27,000** | $18,000–$36,000 |
| $4,000 | **$36,000** | $24,000–$48,000 |
| $5,000 | **$45,000** | $30,000–$60,000 |
| $6,000 | **$54,000** | $36,000–$72,000 |

Count necessities only: property taxes and insurance, utilities, groceries, healthcare, transportation, childcare, minimum debt payments, and essential home maintenance. Keep this money liquid in an FDIC-insured high-yield savings account—not invested in stocks.

**Recommendation:** multiply the family’s essential monthly spending by **9**. For example, at $4,000 per month, hold **$36,000**. Use the upper end—12 months—if the employed spouse’s job is unstable, health coverage is costly, or major home repairs are likely. Race does not affect the calculation.

This is educational guidance; a qualified financial professional can tailor it to the household’s benefits, insurance, and job stability.
